title: The Question
bullets: For the entire span of recorded human history before 1800, the material standard of living of an ordinary person barely moved — a peasant in Song China, Rome, or 1700 England lived at roughly the same level.
Around 1760 in Britain, income per head began compounding upward and never came back down, ultimately making the average human being ten to twenty times richer within two centuries.

The one-sentence physical answer is energy: every prior economy was capped by this year's sunlight mediated through fields, forests, and muscle; coal is three-hundred-million-year-old stored sunlight with no such ceiling.

Around 1700, India produced roughly a quarter of global manufacturing output and China's Song-era iron output matched early-eighteenth-century Britain — so the puzzle is not why others never tried but why self-sustaining growth ignited in this specific corner at this specific moment.

Arnold Toynbee, the young Oxford historian who died at 31 in 1883, gave the period its name in posthumously published lectures, making the era characteristically one that was named by the first generation to finish living through it.

The cotton that built Manchester was picked overwhelmingly by enslaved people in the American South; the early factory workforce was heavily women and children as young as five working twelve-to-fourteen-hour days — both ledgers must be kept open from the start.

title: The Preconditions
bullets: The four-field rotation pioneered by men like Robert Bakewell roughly doubled English agricultural output over the century while releasing labour for the factories — no steam required, just knowledge and reorganization.

Parliamentary enclosure acts extinguished common-land rights and turned a class of poor-but-independent villagers into people with nothing to sell but their labour, which is precisely the workforce a factory needs.

Britain had burned through its forests long before the rest of Europe and was already culturally and technically comfortable with coal by 1700, producing the large mining industry — and the desperate need to pump flooded mines — that drove the steam engine.

Robert Allen's high-wage economy thesis argues that uniquely expensive British labour and uniquely cheap British coal made labour-saving machinery pay in Lancashire when it would have been worthless in low-wage Bengal.

Britain's patent system let inventors like James Watt and Richard Arkwright turn ideas into legally defensible fortunes, and its relatively open society let a barber's apprentice or an instrument-maker become rich rather than staying in their place.

Atlantic slavery supplied the raw material and a vast captive market simultaneously, and historians still argue whether this was central or secondary to the domestic factors — the book's position is that you do not have to pick.

title: The Thread
bullets: In the pre-industrial system it took roughly six spinners working flat out to keep one weaver supplied with yarn, so John Kay's 1733 flying shuttle, by doubling weaving speed, made the yarn shortage catastrophically worse and made the prize for a spinning solution enormous.

James Hargreaves's 1764 spinning jenny let one worker spin eight then sixteen threads at once; Richard Arkwright's 1769 water frame produced thread strong enough for warp; Samuel Crompton's 1779 spinning mule combined both and dominated cotton spinning for a century.

Arkwright's real genius was not the water frame but the system — the integration of powered machinery, waged shift labour, and managerial discipline into a single reproducible factory that could be copied valley by valley, making him arguably the most consequential businessman in the book.

British raw cotton consumption rose by a factor of roughly fifty between the 1780s and the 1850s, and Manchester grew from a modest market town into Cottonopolis, the planet's first true industrial city.

Eli Whitney's 1793 cotton gin made short-staple cotton wildly profitable across the entire Deep South, making American slavery not a declining inheritance but a booming, expanding system that grew in lockstep with the British mills' appetite.

The same machine that enriched Manchester was simultaneously fed by slavery in Mississippi and destroying the finest hand-weavers in Bengal — a Manchester mill-owner and a Mississippi enslaver were two ends of one machine.

title: The Engine
bullets: Thomas Newcomen's 1712 beam engine reliably pumped water out of mines but was so fuel-inefficient it was only worth running at a coal pit, where the fuel was effectively free — that was the steam engine's ceiling for sixty years.

James Watt's key insight, patented in 1769, was the separate condenser: cool the steam in a different vessel so the working cylinder stays hot, roughly tripling fuel efficiency and transforming steam from a mining curiosity into a technology that could pay almost anywhere.

Matthew Boulton provided what Watt lacked — capital, salesmanship, and markets — and Boulton & Watt became the prototype of every science-based engineering company since, with one partner generating cleverness and the other generating customers.

Watt's 1781 rotary motion patent was the moment cotton mills stopped needing rivers: a factory now needed a Boulton & Watt engine and a coal supply and could be built anywhere — in the middle of a town, next to the workers, next to the canal.

To sell engines Boulton and Watt needed a unit of comparison, so Watt measured a dray horse's lifting power over time and coined the horsepower — a piece of marketing arithmetic still used to rate car engines two and a half centuries later.

When Watt's main patent expired in 1800 and the field opened to rivals, the question was no longer whether steam would power industry but how fast, how far, and at what cost to the people fed into the machines.

title: Black Country
bullets: Abraham Darby's 1709 Coalbrookdale breakthrough — smelting iron with coke instead of charcoal — unchained ironmaking from the shrinking English forests and welded it permanently to the coalfield.
Henry Cort's 1784 puddling process let Britain produce good structural wrought iron in bulk using coal, and British iron output multiplied many times over within a few decades.

Song China's iron output around 1078 CE has been estimated at over 100,000 tons a year — roughly what early-eighteenth-century Britain produced — yet that industry hit a wood ceiling and stopped; Darby and Cort's achievement was marrying iron to the limitless coal seam.

Humphry Davy's 1815 safety lamp let miners go deeper into gassier seams, simultaneously saving lives and extending the frontier of danger — coal mining remained one of the deadliest jobs in the world until the 1842 Mines Act.

Henry Maudslay's screw-cutting lathe around 1800 and Joseph Whitworth's 1841 national standard for screw threads meant a bolt made in one city would fit a nut made in another — the hidden hinge on which mass production turns.

The Industrial Revolution's deepest secret is not any single famous machine: it is that a society learned to make machines that make machines, with standardized parts that could be strangers to one another and still fit.

title: The Clock
bullets: Pre-industrial workers followed the rhythm of the task and the season, with widespread artisan traditions like Saint Monday — not working on Mondays to recover from the weekend — which the factory was specifically built to abolish.

A steam engine and a thousand pounds of machinery bleed money whenever idle, so every worker had to be present, synchronized, and producing at the same moment: the locked gate, the bell, and the fine enforced a new kind of time discipline never before imposed on human beings at scale.

In the early mills, children — including pauper apprentices shipped in batches from distant workhouses — worked twelve to sixteen hours a day in heat and cotton dust, beaten with a leather strap to stay awake, their testimony taken on oath by Parliament's own committees in 1832.

The structural trap was that a mill-owner who shortened hours while rivals did not would simply be undercut and ruined — kindly and brutal owners produced about the same suffering, because the machine, not the man, set the terms.

Robert Owen ran the New Lanark mills from 1800 with no children under ten, decent housing, schooling, and a sick fund — and made a healthy profit — proving that the cruelty was a choice the system rewarded, not a law of nature it obeyed.

Owen's followers — the Owenites — founded the early cooperative shops and helped seed the union movement, so a strand of British socialism still bears his name even though the cheap, brutal mill, not New Lanark, became the template.

title: The Arteries
bullets: A single horse that can drag one ton on an 1750s English road can drag thirty tons in a barge on a canal — the physics made waterways the only practical solution for moving coal, iron, and grain in bulk.

The Duke of Bridgewater's 1761 canal from his Worsley coal mines into Manchester roughly halved the price of coal in the city within a few years, proving canals were a license to print money and triggering Canal Mania in the 1790s.
Canal Mania followed the boom-bubble-bust-but-the-infrastructure-survives pattern that Railway Mania would repeat almost beat for beat in the 1840s — a characteristic rhythm of industrial capitalism.

Thomas Telford and John McAdam genuinely improved English roads, and McAdam's cheap crushed-stone surface survives half-transformed in the word tarmac, but turnpikes could never solve the central problem of heavy bulk freight.

The canals were dug by hundreds of thousands of navvies — short for navigators — who worked with pick, barrow, and gunpowder in conditions deadlier than coal mining and died in numbers nobody bothered to count.
The machine age's own infrastructure was built almost entirely by muscle, and the men who built it were among the most exploited and least remembered workers of the entire era.

title: The Iron Horse
bullets: Richard Trevithick ran the first steam locomotive hauling a load on rails in 1804 at a Welsh ironworks, and then the world ignored it for twenty years because the rails shattered under the weight.

George Stephenson's Rocket won the 1829 Rainhill Trials at around thirty miles an hour with two innovations — a multi-tube boiler and a blast pipe — that virtually every steam locomotive for the next century refined.

The Liverpool and Manchester Railway opened in 1830 as the first modern railway with double track, scheduled services, and central control between two major cities; its opening day also produced the age's first famous railway death, the Cabinet minister William Huskisson, run down by the Northumbrian.

Railway Mania in the 1840s followed Canal Mania's exact script — wild over-investment, a crash that ruined thousands, and a superb national network left behind that the country could never have planned so fast.

Isambard Kingdom Brunel built the Great Western Railway with deliberately theatrical audacity — the longest tunnel in the world at Box, a broader gauge for a smoother ride, and the steamship Great Britain to carry passengers onward to New York.

Every town had kept its own solar time until a single train timetable had to be true in Bristol and London simultaneously; the railways imposed a single railway time across the country, and the world has lived on network-defined clock time, not solar time, ever since.

title: The Workshop of the World
bullets: By 1851 Britain produced roughly half the world's iron and cotton cloth, burned more coal than any other nation, and had become the first society in human history where more people lived in towns than in the countryside.

The repeal of the Corn Laws in 1846, with the Irish Famine making cheap imported food politically unanswerable, was the moment the landed gentry — England's governing class for centuries — lost decisively to the men who owned the mills.
Richard Cobden and John Bright built the Anti-Corn Law League into arguably the first modern single-issue political machine, with paid organizers, mass mailings, and rented halls.

David Ricardo's theory of comparative advantage — the counter-intuitive argument that two countries both grow richer if each specializes in whatever it makes relatively best — gave free trade a rigorous spine, though free trade was also nakedly the class interest of manufacturers who wanted cheap food and open foreign markets.

The Crystal Palace, designed by head gardener Joseph Paxton and assembled in months from thousands of identical prefabricated parts, was itself the greatest exhibit inside it: a building that announced the principle of the rest of the century.

Six million visitors — a third of Britain's population — came by the new railways to the Great Exhibition of 1851, but the Crystal Palace stood in a London where, a short walk away, the people who made the wealth were dying of cholera in courts with no clean water.

title: The Price
bullets: Manchester, Leeds, and Glasgow did not grow — they erupted, doubling and redoubling in decades with no sewers, no clean water, and no authority responsible for any of it, because the dominant ideology held such things were not the government's business.

Back-to-back houses sharing rear walls with no ventilation and no yard, at densities where a single outdoor privy served dozens of families and courts ran with sewage, were the physical reality behind the Crystal Palace's coronation.

Friedrich Engels, sent to help run his family's Manchester mill, published a street-by-street indictment of the city in The Condition of the Working Class in England in 1845 — then put what he saw to world-historical use with Marx.

John Snow proved in 1854 that cholera spread through water by plotting every death in a Soho outbreak on a street map and seeing the dots cluster around one pump on Broad Street, founding modern epidemiology.

The Great Stink of 1858 — summer sewage so foul that Parliament's riverside curtains were soaked in chloride of lime — finally forced Joseph Bazalgette's vast London sewer system, which quietly saved more lives than any drug of the century.

The most defensible reading of the standard-of-living evidence is the bleak compromise: the Industrial Revolution made humanity vastly richer in the long run while the first few generations of industrial workers paid for it with their bodies, and a child born in a Manchester cellar in 1830 very probably had a shorter, sicker life than her great-grandmother on a pre-enclosure farm.

Average adult height in army recruits and convicts fell in the worst industrial towns during the worst decades — the clearest single evidence that childhood nutrition and health got worse before they got better.

Edwin Chadwick's 1842 report linking filth to early death, the first Public Health Act in 1848, and Bazalgette's sewers form the unglamorous heroism that actually made the giant city survivable — none of it arrived until the crisis was already catastrophic.

title: The Machine Breakers
bullets: The original Luddites of 1811 to 1816 were skilled textile workers being deliberately destroyed by employers using new machinery to drive down wages and replace skilled men with cheap unskilled labour and children — machine-breaking was collective bargaining by riot in a country where unions were illegal.

Parliament made machine-breaking a capital crime in 1812, and the poet Lord Byron's maiden speech was a furious attack on the bill; at the peak the government deployed more soldiers against the Luddites than Wellington then had fighting Napoleon in Spain.

At Peterloo in August 1819, mounted cavalry charged a peaceful crowd of sixty thousand at St Peter's Field in Manchester — including many women and children in their Sunday best — killing around eighteen and injuring hundreds.
The government answered public horror at Peterloo not with inquiry but with the Six Acts, a battery of laws further restricting public meetings and the radical press.
The Combination Acts of 1799 and 1800 made it a crime for workers to form unions or strike while leaving employers entirely free to agree among themselves to cap wages or blacklist troublemakers.

Six Dorset farm labourers — the Tolpuddle Martyrs — were transported to Australia in 1834 merely for swearing a secret union oath; the mass protest that followed eventually won their pardon and entered the founding mythology of the labour movement.

The handloom weavers were the era's clearest losers: once relatively prosperous skilled men whose entire trade was strangled by the power loom over a generation, they starved by the tens of thousands while the economists explained that this was efficient.

title: The Charter
bullets: By 1830 vast new industrial cities like Manchester and Birmingham had no Members of Parliament at all while rotten boroughs — in one case an empty field — still returned MPs appointed by a single controlling landlord.

The Great Reform Act of 1832 abolished the worst rotten boroughs and gave seats to the industrial cities, but tied the new vote to a property qualification that enfranchised mill-owners and shopkeepers while pointedly leaving the working class exactly where it had been.

The People's Charter of 1838 demanded six points — votes for all adult men, secret ballots, no property qualification for MPs, payment for MPs, equal constituencies, and annual parliaments — five of which are simply how every modern democracy now works.

Chartism was a genuine mass movement with a national newspaper, the Northern Star, which was for a time the best-selling paper outside London, and mass torchlight meetings addressed by Feargus O'Connor to crowds of tens of thousands.
Chartism surged in the bad economic years and ebbed in the good ones, making it the political voice of the hunger described in the previous chapter rather than an abstract constitutional campaign.

Chartism's 1848 climax at Kennington Common was met by rain, a vast pre-positioned military presence, and a government determined to deny it a confrontation; the movement faded — yet every one of its six points except annual parliaments is now law, conceded piece by grudging piece over the seventy years that followed.

The secret ballot came in 1872, payment of MPs in 1911 — each extension wrenched out by pressure and fear, never given freely, proving that political democracy was not a gift of enlightened elites but a demand the industrial working class made against fierce resistance.

title: The Conscience
bullets: Michael Sadler's 1832 parliamentary inquiry printed sworn testimony of children beaten awake at five in the morning to work sixteen-hour days at age seven — making the cruelty legible to people who would never enter a mill in a way abstract argument never could.

The Factory Act of 1833 was the first with teeth: paid factory inspectors could walk in unannounced to enforce a ban on children under nine in textile mills, the moment the British state first accepted that what an employer did to a worker inside a private factory was a legitimate concern of the law.
The 1842 Mines and Collieries Act, prompted by royal commission illustrations of women and children hauling coal trucks on hands and knees, banned women and young children from underground work.

Lord Shaftesbury spent half a century in Parliament as the single most persistent champion of the factory child, the woman in the mine, the chimney sweep, and the pauper lunatic, refusing office to stay free to push reform after reform from explicitly evangelical motives.

When slavery was abolished in 1833, the British government paid £20 million — roughly a fifth of the national budget, on a loan only finally repaid in 2015 — entirely to the slave-owners; the enslaved received nothing.

The Clapham Sect around William Wilberforce invented the template for every subsequent reform movement: the mass petition, the consumer boycott, the parliamentary lobby, the moral argument pressed until it became unanswerable.

Charles Dickens serialized Oliver Twist in 1838 and Hard Times later at a price cheap enough to be read aloud in workshops, moving millions of readers with fictional cruelty in a way that dry statistics on the standard-of-living debate never could.

title: The Argument in Theory
bullets: Thomas Malthus argued in 1798 that population grows faster than food supply, so any improvement in the lot of the poor will simply be eaten up by more surviving children until misery restores the balance — making poverty natural and not the rich man's problem.
The New Poor Law of 1834 herded the destitute into punitive workhouses designed to be worse than the worst job — laissez-faire doctrine translated directly into policy, with a body count.

Roughly a million people starved to death in Ireland in the 1840s while officials citing political economy restrained relief and let grain be exported from the starving country, and the same reasoning turned crop failures into mass death in British India.

John Stuart Mill argued that while the production of wealth might obey iron economic laws, its distribution was a human choice fixed by institutions and politics, not nature — the conceptual hinge on which every later argument for redistribution and the welfare state would swing.
The Rochdale Pioneers, a group of Lancashire weavers, opened a jointly owned store in 1844 sharing its profits among members, becoming the model copied by the worldwide cooperative movement.

The Communist Manifesto, published by Marx and Engels in February 1848 — weeks before revolution swept the Continent — was partly a hymn to capitalism's revolutionary dynamism and partly its deepest structural critique, produced inside the system it analyzed.

Marx wrote Capital's first volume in 1867 in the reading room of the British Museum while Friedrich Engels ran a Manchester cotton mill and funded him, making the workshop of the world the location where the theory to overthrow it was written.

More human beings would live, kill, die, and be governed under regimes claiming Marx's name in the twentieth century than under any other idea in this entire series — in Russia, and above all in China.

title: It Crosses the Channel
bullets: Britain made it illegal to export new machinery or let skilled mechanics emigrate, but knowledge walks out on two legs: the English mechanic William Cockerill slipped across and his son John built an enormous integrated coal-iron-engine complex at Seraing near Liège that made Belgium the first industrialized nation after Britain.
Belgium could go first on the Continent for a very British-sounding reason: rich coal seams sitting right next to iron ore and navigable water — the same lucky geology in miniature.

Friedrich List argued in 1841 that a developing nation must use protective tariffs to shelter its infant industries until they are strong enough to compete, and that free trade is a policy which conveniently suits whoever is already on top.

The Zollverein, a customs union created under Prussian leadership in 1834, turned dozens of separate German sovereign states into a single tariff-free market decades before political unification in 1871 — demonstrating that the political organization of an economy could be deliberately engineered as a precondition for industrial growth.

The Krupp works at Essen grew from a small Ruhr foundry into the largest heavy-industrial enterprise in Europe, fused intimately with the Prussian state, and embodies everything distinctive about Continental industrialization: state direction, banks tightly bound to industry, and a frank orientation toward national power rather than private profit.

France industrialized more slowly and unevenly, keeping far more peasantry and small craft workshops — partly because the Revolution had left France a country of small landowning peasants with every reason to stay on their own land rather than flood into mills.

There was never one inevitable industrial revolution every country went through on schedule: there was a British original and a series of conscious, state-engineered responses to it — and the German response would, within sixty years, overtake the country that wrote the original.

title: It Crosses the Atlantic
bullets: Samuel Slater memorized an Arkwright-system mill's machinery — carrying out drawings was a crime — emigrated in 1789, and rebuilt it from memory in Rhode Island: American industry began as British intellectual property walking off in one man's head.

The Lowell system built whole integrated cotton towns in Massachusetts staffed by young farm women in supervised boarding houses, then within a generation replaced them with cheaper, more desperate Irish immigrant labour under conditions close to the Manchester the system had been built to avoid.

Federal armouries like Springfield and Harpers Ferry, answering America's chronic labour shortage, pushed the interchangeable-parts principle further and faster than anyone, running a direct technological line from New England gun shops to the sewing machine, the bicycle, and Henry Ford's 1913 moving assembly line.

By the 1850s the American economy was two coupled engines running on incompatible fuel: an industrializing, free-labour, protectionist North and a slave-labour, cotton-exporting, free-trade South, and the cotton bound them and the world together.

The Union's victory in the Civil War was substantially a victory of railways, ironworks, and mass-produced weapons over an agrarian society — an early demonstration that industrial capacity now decided wars.

The Union blockade created the Lancashire Cotton Famine, idling British mills and throwing hundreds of thousands of workers into destitution — yet mass meetings of those same workers voted to support the anti-slavery North anyway, and Lincoln publicly thanked them for choosing principle over bread.
The Civil War broke the slave engine; the industrial engine it left running made the United States the largest economy on Earth within thirty years.

title: The Second Revolution
bullets: Henry Bessemer's 1856 process blew air through molten iron to burn off impurities in minutes, making steel cheap enough to replace iron rails, build ships and bridges of a new order of strength, and make possible the steel-framed skyscraper.

William Perkin, trying to synthesize quinine in 1856, accidentally made the first artificial dye from coal-tar waste, and the German chemical industry seized the principle to build the world's first true industrial research laboratories at firms like BASF and Bayer.
Michael Faraday's 1831 discovery of electromagnetic induction — pure curiosity-driven research with no application in sight — became, two generations later, the entire electrical industry.

Edison's Pearl Street station in Manhattan sold electricity commercially for the first time in 1882; Tesla and Westinghouse then won the war of the currents because alternating current could be stepped up to high voltage and sent hundreds of miles, while Edison's direct current faded within a mile.
Karl Benz's 1886 petrol-driven motor car and Rudolf Diesel's compression-ignition engine gave industry a compact, portable power source that needed no boiler, no rails, and no fixed track.

The science of thermodynamics — the deep physics of heat and energy worked out by Carnot, Joule, and Kelvin between the 1820s and 1850s — was developed substantially by men trying to understand steam engines, technology handing a law back to science which then handed a hundred new technologies forward.

The Second Revolution belonged to whoever organized science and the state best, and that was not going to be the country that invented the whole thing — Britain's practical-man-beats-the-professor culture, genuinely right for 1830, was genuinely wrong for 1890.

title: The New Powers
bullets: Around 1890 the United States quietly became the largest economy on Earth, a position it has not relinquished since, driven by a continent of resources, a vast tariff-protected internal market, and the labour-scarcity obsession with mass production.

German universal banks took long-term shareholdings in industrial firms and sat on their boards as partners rather than arm's-length creditors, financing decade-long expansion in ways that British finance-first culture never did.
By the 1900s German steel, chemical, and electrical output had passed Britain's — a country that had started sixty years behind.

The Long Depression of 1873 to the mid-1890s saw two decades of falling prices, collapsing farm incomes, and repeated financial panics that permanently cracked the mid-century article of faith that free trade and minimal government were universal economic law.

The joint-stock company with limited liability unlocked the mass pooling of strangers' savings that giant industry needed; Andrew Carnegie's vertically integrated steel empire, owning iron mines, ore ships, coke ovens, and mills in a single firm, and John D. Rockefeller's Standard Oil trust became the templates for modern industrial concentration.

Frederick Taylor's scientific management sent men with stopwatches onto factory floors to time every motion and strip out the slow and the skilled parts — the clock discipline of the early factory carried to its cold extreme and the immediate ancestor of the assembly line.

The Meiji government from 1868 built model railways, mills, mines, and shipyards, then sold them to favoured zaibatsu conglomerates, compressing Britain's century of industrialization into about three decades — the only non-Western society to industrialize in the nineteenth century.

When Japan's modernized navy destroyed China's in 1894, it proved that industrialization was an institutional transformation, not a shopping list of machines — the lesson twentieth-century China would eventually study hardest of all.

title: The World Remade
bullets: The late nineteenth century produced the first mass-consumption society: the department store, branded packaged goods, ready-made clothing, the bicycle, refrigerated meat shipped across oceans, and a working family that could own more than a medieval lord.

The first globalization ran on steamships, the 1866 transatlantic telegraph cable that sent news across the ocean in minutes instead of weeks, and the gold standard that made a pound, a dollar, and a mark different names for amounts of the same metal.

Tens of millions of Europeans crossed to the industrial New World in the largest voluntary migration in human history, and for the first time populations grew enormously and got richer at the same time, springing the Malthusian trap that had capped every prior economy.

British colonial policy taxed Indian textiles into Britain while letting British cloth into India nearly free, deindustrializing a region that had been the world's greatest textile exporter in the eighteenth century; Bengal's income per head slid backward across the nineteenth century.

When China declined to buy British goods, Britain balanced its books by force, fighting the Opium Wars to compel China to accept the drug at gunpoint — the decisive weapon being the iron, steam-powered shallow-draft gunboat Nemesis, which could go where no Chinese war junk could follow.

The Maxim gun — the first fully automatic machine gun — let a few industrial soldiers hold lethal superiority over a charging army, and the industrial powers used it to carve up nearly the entire continent of Africa in a single generation in the 1880s and 1890s.

Britain alone accounted for something close to half of all world cumulative industrial carbon emissions before 1850 while the societies it dominated had emitted almost nothing — the carbon ledger and the colonial ledger are the same ledger.

In 1896 the Swedish chemist Svante Arrhenius calculated with pencil and paper that doubling atmospheric carbon dioxide would warm the planet by roughly five degrees Celsius — an estimate that sits, unnervingly, in the range climate scientists still give today.

title: The Argument
bullets: The gradualists argue that year-on-year growth statistics are too slow and uneven to deserve the word revolution; the book's position is that the kind of thing an economy was — its relationship to energy — broke permanently, and that break deserves the strong word even if any single decade looks slow from inside.

The bleak compromise on the standard-of-living debate: the Industrial Revolution made the human species incomprehensibly richer in the long run and very probably made the first several generations of industrial workers measurably worse off in their own lifetimes — both halves are supported by serious evidence, neither cancels the other.

Kenneth Pomeranz's California School argues the Yangzi delta around 1750 was not meaningfully behind the most advanced regions of Europe, and that what tipped Britain was substantially coal that happened to lie next to its workshops and New World colonies supplying food and fibre its own soil could never have grown.

Robert Allen's high-wage economy thesis argues that uniquely expensive British labour and uniquely cheap British coal made labour-saving machines pay in Lancashire while they would have been worthless in low-wage Bengal.
Joel Mokyr emphasizes a peculiar Enlightenment marriage of science with useful knowledge in a society that uniquely rewarded the practical innovator rather than ignoring or suppressing him.

The one answer that is definitely wrong is that the British were simply more brilliant — the societies that did not industrialize first were not less intelligent or less inventive and were in several cases more so for most of recorded history.

The Industrial Revolution handed the twentieth century the factory and the corporation; the working class and the vote and the welfare state, wrenched out by the pressure of Chartism and the machine-breakers; and the rival faiths of liberal capitalism and Marxist socialism that would divide the planet and arm it.

The carbon put into the atmosphere since the early 1700s is the one bequest still growing — the gift and the bill fused into a single physical fact that every later chapter of human history must be written around, addressed unmistakably to everyone now alive.
